hallucinations.description: The paper cites "Roman et al. (2023). Open Datasheets: Machine-readable documentation for open datasets and responsible AI assessments. arXiv preprint arXiv:2312.06153." This reference cannot be verified from the provided materials, and the paper relies on it for key background claims about a HuggingFace Open Datasheets system and a two-tier lifecycle structure. Given the instruction to focus on nonexistent citations, this citation is suspect and appears hallucinated relative to the evidence available in the code and task materials.
hallucinations.evidence: "Roman, A., et al. (2023). Open Datasheets: Machine-readable documentation for open datasets and responsible AI assessments. arXiv preprint arXiv:2312.06153."

hallucinations.description: The paper claims an annotation protocol involving three expert annotators and reports inter-annotator agreement, but the code shows no human annotation workflow. Instead, the experiment generates synthetic/content-based annotations automatically. Therefore, the methodology as written is misleading: it presents human annotation as part of the study design while the implementation used simulated labels.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper: "The original experimental plan specified that three expert annotators would independently label all 300 fields... However, the actual implementation used content-based heuristics to simulate annotations rather than human expert labeling." Code/log: "Generating realistic content-based annotations..." and "data.annotation_protocol - INFO - Generating synthetic annotations with target κ=0.65..."

hallucinations.description: The paper reports detailed cross-repository linear probe accuracies (Unscaffolded HuggingFace 82.7%, OpenML 79.0%, UCI 76.0%, Overall 79.6%) and class-specific precision/recall. However, the provided h-e1 code only trains and evaluates a probe on a small scaffolded HuggingFace split (60 train, 15 test) and logs only a single probe accuracy/F1. No code or result file supports the reported cross-repository evaluation table or the Responsible AI precision/recall values.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper: "Cross-repository testing on held-out data yielded: Unscaffolded HuggingFace 82.7%, OpenML 79.0%, UCI 76.0%, Overall 79.6%... For the Responsible AI class... 77.8% recall with 66.7% precision." Code: run_experiment.py performs only "Training linear probe: 60 train, 15 test" on scaffolded data; experiment.log reports only "Probe Accuracy: 0.867" and "Probe F1: 0.830" with no repository-wise breakdown.

hallucinations.description: The paper reports K-means NMI as a mean over 10 random seeds with standard deviation (0.0229 ± 0.0031 across 10 random seeds). The integrated code uses a single fixed random state for K-means and the saved results file contains only one semantic NMI value. There is no evidence of a 10-seed repeated experiment or variance estimation.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper: "K-means clustering with k=2 achieved mean NMI of 0.0229 (±0.0031 across 10 random seeds)." Code: clustering config sets "random_state: int = 42" and run_experiment.py calls clustering once. Results file: "semantic": 0.02293982755990053.

hallucinations.description: The repository-stratified results in the paper do not match the actual saved outputs. The paper reports HuggingFace NMI 0.013 and OpenML NMI 0.051, but the results JSON only contains HuggingFace 0.025354... and UCI 0.391422..., with no OpenML NMI at all. Likewise, the paper reports repository-specific probe accuracies for all three repositories, while the results JSON contains only HuggingFace and UCI probe results.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper: "Repository-specific clustering performance... UCI 0.394, OpenML 0.051, HuggingFace 0.013" and "Probe Accuracy... UCI 91.3%, OpenML 88.7%, HuggingFace 95.8%." Results JSON: "repository_nmis": {"HuggingFace": 0.025354447720559965, "UCI": 0.39142233882156474} and "probe_results": {"HuggingFace": 0.9733333333333333, "UCI": 1.0}.

hallucinations.description: The paper's scaffolding-effect results conflict with the actual experiment outputs. The paper states scaffolded NMI 0.018, unscaffolded NMI 0.011, and a positive gap of 0.007, but the saved results show scaffolded 0.0471, unscaffolded 0.0591, and a negative gap of -0.0120. The paper also reports scaffolded/unscaffolded probe accuracies that are not produced by the code.
hallucinations.evidence: Paper: "Scaffolded (n=75) | NMI 0.018 | Probe Accuracy 98.7%; Unscaffolded (n=75) | NMI 0.011 | Probe Accuracy 94.7%; Gap | 0.007 | 4.0pp." Results JSON: "scaffolding_results": {"scaffolded": 0.04705165318656599, "unscaffolded": 0.05907830953001798, "gap": -0.012026656343451993}.

hallucinations.description: The paper interprets the minority-cluster size as implying "K-means achieves approximately 52% (13/25)" for Responsible AI recall. This is not mathematically justified. A cluster of size 13 does not imply that 13 of the 25 Responsible AI items were correctly identified; cluster labels are unlabeled and cluster composition must be examined before computing recall. The paper infers a class recall directly from cluster size, which is incorrect.
hallucinations.evidence: "Supervised probes overcome imbalance through learned class weights, achieving 77.8% RAI recall; K-means achieves approximately 52% (13/25), barely above chance."

hallucinations.description: The paper states that K-means NMI 0.0229 "represents 4% of the 0.60 threshold target" and elsewhere that it is "2.3% NMI normalized to percentage scale." These percentage interpretations are inconsistent and conceptually sloppy. 0.0229 as a percentage is 2.29%, whereas 0.0229/0.60 ≈ 3.82% of the threshold. Mixing these interpretations without distinction is mathematically misleading.
hallucinations.evidence: "K-means clustering with k=2 achieved mean NMI of 0.0229... This represents 4% of the 0.60 threshold target." and later "a 77-percentage-point gap between supervised probe accuracy (79.6%) and unsupervised clustering performance (2.3% NMI normalized to percentage scale)."

overall_assessment: Hallucinations were found. The most serious issues are fabricated or unsupported experimental results: the paper reports numerous repository-specific, cross-repository, multi-seed, and class-specific metrics that are not produced by the provided code or saved results and in several cases directly contradict them. There are also methodological misrepresentations around annotation and at least one suspect citation, plus some flawed mathematical interpretations.
